items.note: Verb (て-form) + から = the first action must be FULLY COMPLETE before the second begins. 「シャワーを浴びてから、朝ごはんを食べます」 = After taking a shower, I eat breakfast. Different from 〜ながら (simultaneous) and 〜前に (before). 「日本語を勉強してから、日本に来ました」 = I came to Japan after studying Japanese. The order is always: てから action FIRST, then the result.

Verb STEM (drop ます) + ながら = doing two things at the same time. 「音楽を聞きながら、散歩します」 = I walk while listening to music. The ながら action is the BACKGROUND activity; the main verb is the foreground. You CAN'T use ながら for two equal, sequential actions (use てから). 「食べながら、テレビを見ます」 = I watch TV while eating. Very common in everyday speech.

Verb (て-form) + みる = 'do it and see'. みる comes from 見る (to see/look). 「この料理を食べてみてください！」 = Please try eating this dish! 「日本語で話してみました」 = I tried speaking in Japanese. It implies curiosity, experimentation, or tentative attempt — not obligation. 食べてみる (try eating) vs ただ食べる (just eat). One of the most useful N4 patterns for travel.

Verb (て-form) + おく = prepare/do something now for future convenience. おく = to place/put. 「旅行の前に、ホテルを予約しておきました」 = I booked the hotel in advance (before the trip). 「窓を開けておきます」 = I'll leave the window open (for a reason). Casual speech: ておく → とく (食べとく, 見とく). The nuance is always about doing something with future purpose.

Verb (て-form) + しまう = two meanings. (1) REGRET: 「傘を忘れてしまいました！」 = I unfortunately forgot my umbrella! (2) COMPLETION: 「宿題をやってしまいました」 = I finished all the homework (and it's done). Context makes it clear. Casual: てしまう → ちゃう / でしまう → じゃう. 「食べちゃった！」 = I ate it all up! Very common in spoken Japanese.

english: come to be able to ~ / reached the point where ~
items.note: Verb (dict form or potential form) + ようになる = a change that happened gradually over time. 「日本語が少し話せるようになりました！」 = I've reached the point where I can speak a little Japanese! Expresses a NEW ABILITY or habit that didn't exist before. Compare: 話せます (I can speak [now]) vs 話せるようになりました (I came to be able to speak [process]). 食べなかった魚が食べられるようになった = I grew to be able to eat fish (that I couldn't before).

english: make it a habit to ~ / make sure to ~
items.note: Verb (dict form / negative) + ようにする = deliberately trying to maintain a habit or ensure something happens. 「毎日日本語を勉強するようにしています」 = I make it a point to study Japanese every day. 「遅刻しないようにしています」 = I make sure not to be late. ようになる = describes what HAPPENED (change occurred). ようにする = describes your ongoing EFFORT (active). Both appear in N4 JLPT questions.

Verb STEM / Adj STEM + そうです = based on something you directly SEE or observe. 「雨が降りそうです」 = It looks like it will rain (you see dark clouds). 「このケーキ、おいしそう！」 = This cake looks delicious! EXCEPTION: いい → よさそう (not いそう). 高い → 高そう. Note: this uses the STEM, not the full plain form. Compare with そうです② below which uses the full form and means 'I heard that~'.

PLAIN FORM (全体) + そうです = something you heard from someone else. 「田中さんが来るそうです」 = I heard that Tanaka-san is coming. 「天気予報によると、明日は雨が降るそうです」 = According to the weather forecast, it seems it will rain tomorrow. Uses the COMPLETE plain form (not the stem). Contrast with そうです① (appearance): 雨が降りそう (looks like rain, you see clouds) vs 雨が降るそう (I heard it will rain).

Plain form + でしょう (polite) / だろう (casual) = probably, in my judgement. Two intonations: (1) FALLING: 明日は晴れるでしょう = It will probably be sunny tomorrow. (2) RISING: 知っているでしょう？ = You know, right? / Don't you know? Weather forecasts use でしょう constantly: 「明日の東京の天気は、曇りのち雨でしょう」 = Tomorrow in Tokyo, cloudy then probably rain.

Plain form + かもしれない / かもしれません = 50% or less certainty. Less confident than でしょう. 「彼は来ないかもしれません」 = He might not come. 「この店、もう閉まっているかもしれない」 = This shop might already be closed. Japanese speakers use かもしれない even when fairly sure — it's a cultural softener. Confidence scale: かもしれない (maybe) < でしょう (probably) < に違いない (must be).

english: should be / expected to be (logical expectation)
items.note: Plain form (or na-adj + な) + はずです = logical expectation based on prior knowledge. 「もうすぐ来るはずです」 = She should arrive soon (based on what was arranged). Different from でしょう (general guess). はず implies you KNOW something should be true. Disappointment form: 〜はずだったのに = 'it was supposed to... but...' 「6時に来るはずだったのに、来なかった」 = They were supposed to come at 6 but didn't.

Verb (dict form) + つもりです = a firm personal intention. 「来年、N3を受けるつもりです」 = I intend to take the N3 exam next year. Past: 〜つもりだった = 'I intended to (but didn't)'. Negative intention: 〜ないつもりです = 'I intend not to do ~'. 「今日は飲まないつもりです」 = I don't plan to drink today. Stronger than 〜と思っています (I'm thinking of doing ~).

english: in order to ~ / for the sake of ~
items.note: Verb (dict form) + ために / Noun + のために = PURPOSE. 「日本語を話すために、毎日練習します」 = I practice every day in order to speak Japanese. 「健康のために、野菜を食べます」 = I eat vegetables for my health. KEY RULE: the subjects of both clauses must match for purpose-ために. 「友達のために料理を作った」 = I cooked for my friend (for the sake of = beneficiary). ために also means 'because of' (negative cause): 「雨のために、試合が中止になった」 = The match was cancelled because of rain.

Verb past (た-form) + ら = the most common spoken conditional. Covers 'if', 'when', and 'after'. 「日本に着いたら、連絡してください」 = When you arrive in Japan, please contact me. 「お金があったら、世界旅行をします」 = If I had money, I would travel the world. Also for advice: 「行ったらどうですか？」 = Why don't you go? Result can be past: 「家に帰ったら、手紙が届いていた」 = When I got home, a letter had arrived.

english: it would be better to ~ / you should ~
items.note: Giving advice. POSITIVE: Verb (た-form) + ほうがいいです = you should do ~. 「日焼け止めを塗ったほうがいいですよ」 = You should put on sunscreen! NEGATIVE: Verb (negative plain) + ほうがいい = better not to. 「食べないほうがいい」 = You'd better not eat that. The た-form for positive advice sounds more direct and emphatic. Very common at restaurants: 「この魚、食べたほうがいいですよ、新鮮です！」.

english: have done ~ before / have had the experience of ~
items.note: Verb (た-form) + ことがあります = a life experience, at least once. 「富士山に登ったことがありますか？」 = Have you ever climbed Mt. Fuji? 「ない: 登ったことがありません」 = I have never climbed it. Compare: 食べたことがあります (I have eaten it at some point in my life) vs 食べています (I eat it / I'm eating now). ことがある can also mean 'sometimes': 「仕事でミスすることがある」 = There are times when I make mistakes at work.

Plain form + んです (spoken) / のです (written) = adds explanatory nuance. 「どうしたんですか？」 = What's the matter? → 「お腹が痛いんです」 = The thing is, my stomach hurts. Without ん: 'My stomach hurts' (neutral statement). With ん: 'That's the explanation' (I'm telling you why / sharing context). Also asks for explanation: 「なぜ来なかったんですか？」 = Why is it that you didn't come? Extremely common — dropping ん often sounds unnatural or abrupt.

Verb STEM + 直す = 'fix by doing again'. 直す (naosu) alone = to fix/repair. 「料理を温め直してください」 = Please reheat the food (re-warm it properly). 「書き直す」 = rewrite. 「読み直す」 = reread. 「考え直す」 = reconsider. 「やり直す」 = redo from scratch. The 直す component conveys that the FIRST attempt was insufficient and needs correction. Very useful in restaurants: 「少し冷めたので、温め直してもらえますか？」 = The food got a bit cold — could you reheat it?

english: could I have ~? / would you please ~? (polite)
items.note: Noun + をいただけますか = the most polite way to request something. いただく = humble form of もらう (to receive). いただけますか = 'could I humbly receive ~?' Compare three levels: ①「水をください」 = Give me water (direct, can seem curt). ②「お水をもらえますか？」 = Could I have water? (polite). ③「お水をいただけますか？」 = Could I please have water? (very polite). Use ③ in restaurants, hotels, with strangers. Adding お before the noun (お水、お茶) also adds politeness.

Person に + Verb (て-form) + ほしい = what you want SOMEONE ELSE to do. Compare: 〜たい = what YOU want to do. 「先生にもっとゆっくり話してほしいです」 = I want the teacher to speak more slowly. 「友達に来てほしい」 = I want my friend to come. In practice, you often soften it: 「ゆっくり話していただけますか？」 (more polite: could you speak slowly?). 〜てほしくない = I don't want someone to do ~.

items.script: どちら / どれが〜ですか
english: which one? / what do you prefer?
items.note: どちら = which (of two) — polite. どれ = which (of three or more). 「どちらがお好みですか？」 = Which do you prefer? (lit. 'which is your preference?'). お好み (okonomi) = preference (from 好む, to prefer). 「コーヒーと紅茶、どちらがいいですか？」 = Coffee or black tea, which would you like? Casual: どっちがいい？ Shops and restaurants use どちら constantly. 「お好みで選べます」 = You can choose to your taste.
